overview.BriefSummary: The 2016 Ukraine Electric Power Attack involved a sophisticated campaign targeting energy providers using spear-phishing attachments exploiting known vulnerabilities.

threat_actor_profile.motivation: Sabotage and disruption of critical infrastructure

Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Internet-facing assets, Internal networks, End-user devices

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.kev: CVE-2020-3992 [CWE-22]: Microsoft Office memory corruption vulnerability
stage.description: The attackers crafted convincing spear-phishing emails targeting employees at energy companies. These emails contained malicious attachments exploiting the CVE-2020-3992 vulnerability in Microsoft Office, allowing the execution of arbitrary code.

stage.name: Execution
stage.kev: CVE-2015-4852 [CWE-476]: Microsoft Windows PowerShell remoting client allows remote code execution
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 579 - Replace Winlogon Helper DLL
stage.description: Upon successful exploitation of the attachment, PowerShell was used to execute commands remotely, leveraging CVE-2015-4852 to bypass security restrictions and maintain persistence.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.description: The attackers created scheduled tasks to ensure their malware would run automatically upon reboot, maintaining persistence within the compromised systems.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.description: To escalate privileges, the attackers exploited weaknesses in User Account Control (UAC) to gain higher-level access rights, enabling them to perform further actions undetected.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.description: To evade detection, the attackers signed their malicious code with stolen legitimate certificates, making it appear safe to unsuspecting users and security software.

stage.name: Impact
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 522 - Malicious Hardware Component Replacement
stage.description: The ultimate goal of the attack was to disrupt industrial control systems by manipulating or disabling critical components, leading to power outages and other disruptions.

Scenario.summary: The 2016 Ukraine Electric Power Attack demonstrated the potential impact of targeted campaigns against critical infrastructure, highlighting the importance of robust email security measures and regular patch management.
